perturbation.original_text: C. Subject to the terms and conditions of the applicable content agreement with such Licensor, the Net Advertising Revenue shall be calculated on a quarterly calendar basis (the "Net Advertising Revenue Period") and shall be payable no later than sixty (60) days after the termination of the preceding full quarter-annual period, i.e., commencing on the first (1st) day of January, April, July and October except that the first and last calendar periods may be "short," depending on the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: C. Subject to the terms and conditions of the applicable content agreement with such Licensor, the Net Advertising Revenue shall be calculated on a quarterly calendar basis (the "Net Advertising Revenue Period") and shall be payable no later than one hundred and twenty (120) days after the termination of the preceding full quarter-annual period, i.e., commencing on the first (1st) day of January, April, July and October except that the first and last calendar periods may be "short," depending on the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the payment period to 120 days may violate California's prompt payment laws if the licensors are considered contractors or subcontractors providing services to the company and are based in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code section 7107 states that failure to make payments to contractors/subcontractors within the timeframe established by the parties in the contract is a cause for disciplinary action. While the law does not specify a maximum payment period, a payment period of 120 days may be deemed unreasonable and could lead to disciplinary action if a dispute arises, especially if industry standards suggest shorter payment terms.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 7. Disciplinary Proceedings [7090 - 7124.6] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7107. Abandonment without legal excuse of any construction project or operation engaged in or undertaken by the licensee as a contractor constitutes a cause for disciplinary action. (Added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period to 120 days, which, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, may violate California's prompt payment laws, especially if the licensors are considered contractors or subcontractors. The Law Explanation mentions that California Business and Professions Code section 7107 allows for disciplinary action for failure to make payments within a reasonable timeframe. Scraped Law Snippet 1, taken from BPC 7107, confirms that abandonment of a project or operation constitutes a cause for disciplinary action, strengthening the argument that unreasonable payment delays can also lead to disciplinary measures against the licensee. Since the change introduces a potential violation of California law regarding payment timelines, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: B. All books and records relative to COMPANY'S obligations to a particular Licensor hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to that Licensor for inspection at a location in Los Angeles, California for at least twelve (12) months after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: B. All books and records relative to COMPANY'S obligations to a particular Licensor hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to that Licensor for inspection at a location in Los Angeles, California for at least six (6) months after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to 6 months could violate California's statute of limitations for breach of contract, which is 4 years for written contracts.  If a dispute arises more than 6 months after termination but within the 4-year statute of limitations, the licensor may not be able to access the necessary records.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By limiting the record retention period to six months, the contract impairs the Licensor's ability to effectively pursue a breach of contract claim within the legally permissible four-year window, because critical evidence (the books and records) may no longer be available.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 337 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from 12 months to 6 months, which contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 337. This law establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By shortening the retention period, the licensor's ability to pursue a breach of contract claim within the four-year window is impaired because evidence (books and records) might not be available, creating a legal inconsistency as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The law snippet, 'Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing,' directly supports the four-year statute of limitations, which the modified contract undermines.

perturbation.original_text: A. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other in the event of a breach of a material provision hereof unless, during that thirty (30) day period, the party receiving the notice cures the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: A. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon ten (10) days written notice to the other in the event of a breach of a material provision hereof unless, during that ten (10) day period, the party receiving the notice cures the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days for a material breach may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, particularly if the breach requires significant time and effort to rectify.  California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, and a 10-day cure period may not allow sufficient time to remedy a complex breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this contract is not explicitly for the lease of goods, California Commercial Code § 2A518 (Cover; Lessee's Procurement of Substitute Goods) implies a principle that the aggrieved party (here, the party not in breach) must be afforded a reasonable time to cover or find a substitute. Reducing the cure period to 10 days may be argued as unreasonable, impeding the non-breaching party's ability to mitigate damages and potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2A518 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the cure period from 30 to 10 days, which, according to the perturbation explanation, may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law. The Law Explanation suggests this shorter period could impede the non-breaching party's ability to mitigate damages, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Code, COM 2A518) primarily relates to the lease of goods, the Law Explanation extends the principle of reasonable time to cover or find a substitute to the contract in question. The reduced cure period directly impacts the ability of the non-breaching party to adequately address the breach, creating an inconsistency with the implied covenant of good faith, making the contract term potentially unenforceable, which qualifies as a legal contradiction.
